steps: 1. Users send L2 transactions — fast, cheap, confirmed in ~1 s
2. The Sequencer orders + executes them in a batch
3. The batch is COMPRESSED (calldata → blob) ~10–100× smaller
4. Compressed blob + new state root posted to L1 (EIP-4844 blob lane)
5. L1 verifies the proof and finalises — L2 inherits Ethereum security
rollupsAnim.heading: L2 user txs → Sequencer → Compression → L1 finalisation

svg.l2Sub: Where your txs actually run · fast · cheap
svg.l1Sub: Settlement + data availability · slow · expensive · secure

title.title: Ethereum: From Money to Programmable Blockchain
title.subtitle: Accounts, EVM, smart contracts, consensus evolution, and the EVM ecosystem

why.heading: Why Ethereum?
why.subtitle: Bitcoin's Limits → Vitalik's Insight

limits.title: No loops in Script
limits.detail: Intentional — prevents infinite loops but limits expressiveness

limits.title: No state beyond UTXO
limits.detail: Can't store arbitrary data or run stateful applications

limits.title: No custom logic
limits.detail: Every transaction follows the same spend-output model

limits.title: Every dApp needs its own chain
limits.detail: No shared environment — each project must bootstrap security from scratch

why.quoteA: "What if we built a blockchain that could run

properties.label: Turing-complete scripting language
properties.desc: Smart contracts can express any computation

properties.label: Global shared state (accounts)
properties.desc: One universal ledger for all addresses and contracts

properties.label: Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)
properties.desc: A sandboxed runtime replicated on every node worldwide

why.callout: Ethereum = Bitcoin's security model + programmable state machine
why.teaserTextA: …and in 2022, Ethereum did something Bitcoin can't:
why.teaserNote: we'll cover The Merge in detail later ↓

accountsVisual.heading: EOA vs Contract Account
accountsVisual.subtitle: The key architectural difference — who initiates, who responds

eoa.title: EOA
eoa.subtitle: Externally Owned Account — the MetaMask / hardware wallet you use day-to-day
points: Controlled by a private key (a human or software)
The ONLY type that can initiate a transaction
Holds ETH balance — no code, no storage
eoa.examples: MetaMask wallet · Hardware wallet · Exchange hot wallet

contract.title: Contract Account
contract.subtitle: Smart Contract
points: Controlled by code — no private key exists
Can ONLY respond to incoming calls (never initiates)
Has ETH balance + executable code + persistent storage
Gas is paid by the EOA that triggered it
contract.examples: Uniswap pool · USDC token · NFT collection · DAO treasury

evm.heading: The Ethereum Virtual Machine
evm.subtitle: A deterministic, sandboxed runtime replicated on every Ethereum node
evm.jvmP1B: ? Since the 90s, Java's promise has been
evm.jvmP1D: file to bytecode, and any device with a JVM runs it identically. Windows, Mac, Linux, ATMs, set-top boxes — same bytecode, same result.
evm.jvmP2Strong: The EVM is exactly the same idea — but every node in the world is the JVM, and the program is your smart contract.
evm.jvmP2A: You compile Solidity to EVM bytecode, the network runs it identically on tens of thousands of machines, and they all agree on the result. JVM solved portability; EVM adds

properties.label: Sandboxed
properties.detail: Completely isolated from the host machine — no file system, no network access

properties.label: Deterministic
properties.detail: Same input → same output everywhere, on every node, every time

properties.label: Turing-complete
properties.detail: Can run any computable program (within gas limits)

properties.label: Gas-metered
properties.detail: Every opcode has a fixed gas cost — prevents infinite loops and spam

transaction.heading: Ethereum Transaction Anatomy
transaction.subtitle: Every state change in Ethereum is triggered by a transaction

fields.comment: // total cap = base fee + tip cap

types.label: Simple ETH Transfer
types.detail: to = EOA · data empty · 21,000 gas (fixed cost)

types.label: Contract Call
types.detail: to = contract address · data = function selector + encoded params

types.label: Contract Deployment
types.detail: to = null · data = compiled bytecode · higher gas cost

transaction.noteBody: Ethereum tracks balance directly on each account. No input/output chains — just add/subtract from a global state.

gas.heading: Gas — Paying for Computation
gas.subtitle: Every operation costs gas. Gas links computation to economics — and stops infinite loops cold.
gas.whatBodyB: Every EVM opcode has a fixed gas price:
gas.whatBodyD: costs ~20,000. The harder the work, the more gas it burns.

why.rest: the EVM is Turing-complete — gas guarantees every transaction terminates.
a free chain would be flooded instantly.

gas.footnoteB: (10⁻⁹ ETH). Run out mid-execution and your tx
gas.footnoteC: — but you still pay for the gas already burned.

eip1559.heading: EIP-1559 — Making Fees Predictable
eip1559.subtitle: London hard fork · August 2021 · the upgrade that fixed Ethereum's fee market
eip1559.beforeBody: Users blindly guessed how much to bid. The top fees won block space. Everyone overpaid out of fear of being stuck.
eip1559.tipBodyA: Optional. Paid by user, kept by the
eip1559.tipBodyB: . Usually 1–2 Gwei — only spikes for urgent inclusion.

whyWorks.rest: wallets show base fee in real time — no blind bidding.
burned ETH offsets new issuance — sometimes net negative.
can't pocket base fee, so no incentive to manipulate it.

smartContracts.heading: Smart Contracts on Ethereum
smartContracts.subtitle: Programs stored on-chain that execute deterministically when triggered

lifecycle.label: Write
lifecycle.detail: Author code in Solidity (or Vyper, Huff…)

lifecycle.label: Deploy
lifecycle.detail: Send tx to null address with bytecode as data

lifecycle.label: Interact
lifecycle.detail: Anyone calls functions via the ABI

properties: Immutable after deploy — code cannot be changed once on-chain
Deterministic — same inputs always produce same outputs
Transparent — source code verifiable on Etherscan

consensus.heading: The Merge: PoW → PoS
consensus.subtitle: September 15, 2022 — Ethereum's biggest upgrade

steps.label: Deposit
steps.detail: Validator locks 32 ETH in the official beacon-chain deposit contract. Each validator runs its own keys and client.
steps.metric: 32 ETH min · withdrawals live since Shapella (Apr 2023)

steps.label: Propose
steps.detail: Pseudo-randomly selected via RANDAO to build the next block. Slots run on a fixed 12-second clock — 32 slots = 1 epoch.

steps.label: Attest
steps.detail: Committees of ~128 validators sign BLS-aggregated votes on the head of the chain and on the epoch checkpoint.

steps.label: Finalize
steps.detail: Casper FFG finalises a checkpoint after two consecutive justified epochs. Reverting it would require slashing ≥ 1/3 of all staked ETH.

consensus.slashingBody: Double-signing or surround voting triggers an immediate burn (1 ETH minimum) plus a correlation penalty proportional to other slashings nearby — making coordinated attacks economically catastrophic.
consensus.liquidBody: Lido (stETH), Rocket Pool (rETH) and others let users stake any ETH amount via derivative tokens. Lido alone secures ~30% of all staked ETH — boosting access at the cost of validator-set concentration.

evmEcosystem.heading: EVM Everywhere
evmEcosystem.subtitle: The EVM has become the de-facto standard virtual machine for blockchains

reasons.label: Network effects
reasons.detail: Largest developer community in the blockchain space — millions of Solidity developers, the most audited contracts, the richest ecosystem of protocols

reasons.label: Tooling
reasons.detail: Hardhat, Foundry, Remix, MetaMask, Ethers.js, Viem, OpenZeppelin — a complete, mature developer stack

reasons.label: Composability
reasons.detail: All EVM chains share open standards: ERC-20 (tokens), ERC-721 (NFTs), ERC-4626 (vaults) — protocols can interoperate seamlessly

evmEcosystem.callout: "The EVM is becoming the standard instruction set for blockchains — like x86 for computers"

rollups.heading: Rollups — Scaling Without Trusting
rollups.subtitleB: batch thousands of transactions off-chain and post a single compressed update to L1 — inheriting its security.

steps.label: Users transact on L2
steps.detail: You send a transaction to the rollup — same wallet, same UX. Confirmed in ~1–2 seconds. Fees: cents.

steps.label: Sequencer batches them
steps.detail: Hundreds-to-thousands of L2 transactions get ordered and executed by the rollup's sequencer.

steps.label: Compressed data posted to L1
steps.detail: Compressed batch + new state root → posted to Ethereum (via blobs since EIP-4844). L1 stores the data so anyone can verify.

steps.label: L1 verifies and finalises
steps.detail: A proof (validity / fraud) tells L1 the batch is correct. Once accepted on L1, the rollup state is as secure as Ethereum itself.

rollups.securityNoteRest: · execution moved off-chain · data still on-chain

zk.text: L1 verifies math — no challenge needed

rollups.metricsLabel: L1 vs L2 — what changes for users
rollups.eip4844B: data — a cheap, ephemeral lane on L1 dedicated to rollups. L2 fees dropped ~10× overnight.

rollupsAnimSlide.heading: What Actually Happens Between L1 and L2
rollupsAnimSlide.subtitle: You just read the steps. Now watch them: user tx → sequencer → batch → compressed blob → L1 finalisation.

defi.heading: DeFi — Three Primitives, One Idea
defi.subtitleA: DeFi replaces financial intermediaries with smart contracts. Three primitives explain ~90% of what's out there.
defi.subtitleHighlight: We'll go deeper in Course 03 (Smart Contracts) — this is the lay of the land.

amm.title: AMM
amm.subtitle: Automated Market Maker
amm.body: Two tokens sit in a pool. A formula sets the price automatically — no buyers and sellers to match.

lending.title: Lending
lending.subtitle: Over-Collateralised Loans
lending.body: Supply crypto to earn interest. Borrow against collateral worth more than your loan. No credit score, no KYC. If your collateral falls below threshold, a bot liquidates you.

yield.title: Yield
yield.subtitle: LP tokens · Staking · Vaults
yield.bodyB: to an AMM, stake ETH for validator rewards, or deposit into auto-compounding vaults — earning a share of the fees.
yield.ilRest: · price divergence can leave LPs worse off than just holding.

defi.hook: Almost every DeFi product you'll hear about is a composition of these three. Course 03 covers the contract-level mechanics, attacks, and case studies.

stablecoins.heading: Stablecoins — the most-used app category
stablecoins.subtitle: Tokens engineered to track a stable reference (almost always 1 USD). Quietly the largest dApp category by volume — and the bridge between traditional finance and crypto.
stablecoins.whatBodyA: An on-chain token whose value tracks an external reference — almost always the US dollar. The interesting question is
stablecoins.whatBodyB: : actual dollars in a bank, on-chain crypto collateral, or pure algorithm. Each model fails differently.

cards.title: Fiat-backed
cards.mechanism: A regulated issuer holds USD (or short-dated treasuries) 1:1 in a bank account. Users mint tokens by depositing fiat; redeem by burning tokens. Pegged because the issuer guarantees redemption.
cards.examples: USDT (Tether) · ~$140B — largest, opaque attestations · USDC (Circle) · ~$60B — monthly Big-Four attestations · PYUSD (PayPal) · ~$1B — newer, regulated.
cards.risk: You're trusting the issuer. USDC briefly depegged to $0.87 in March 2023 when Circle had $3.3B locked in collapsed Silicon Valley Bank. Tether has settled multiple lawsuits over reserve disclosures.

cards.title: Crypto-backed
cards.mechanism: Users lock crypto (ETH, wBTC) into a smart contract at a 150-200% collateral ratio and mint stablecoins. If collateral value drops, the contract auto-liquidates positions. No human issuer.
cards.examples: DAI (MakerDAO/Sky) · ~$5B — the original, since 2017 · GHO (Aave) · LUSD (Liquity) · crvUSD (Curve) — each with slightly different liquidation mechanics.
cards.risk: Capital-inefficient (need $150 of crypto to mint $100). Collateral itself is volatile — a fast crash can outrun liquidations (DAI briefly traded over $1 in March 2020). Modern DAI now holds significant USDC reserves, partially re-introducing custodial risk.

cards.title: Algorithmic & hybrid
cards.mechanism: Maintain the peg via supply-mint/burn algorithms or delta-neutral derivatives positions. Pure algorithmic versions have a documented failure mode; hybrids (Frax, Ethena) blend collateral with synthetic strategies.
cards.examples: TerraUSD (UST) · collapsed May 2022, wiped out ~$60B — death-spiral case study · Frax · ~$650M, partially-collateralised hybrid · Ethena USDe · synthetic delta-neutral, ~$5B+ in 2024.
cards.risk: Pure algorithmic: every prior attempt has failed. Hybrid: Ethena depends on ETH-perp funding rates staying positive — works in bull markets, untested through a sustained bear cycle. Treat with extra caution.

stablecoins.scaleBodyB: · daily settlement volume rivals Visa · USDT alone settles more on-chain volume than Bitcoin some days. Stablecoins are the most-used dApp by transaction count.
stablecoins.regBody: EU MiCA (live 2024) restricts non-euro algorithmic stablecoins · US frameworks debated in Congress · Hong Kong sandbox active. Issuer transparency, reserve audits, and licensing are the new baseline.

apps1.heading: Beyond DeFi — what else runs on Ethereum
apps1.subtitle: DeFi is the most-discussed app category — but not the only one. The same EVM hosts ownership, governance, identity, and games at meaningful scale.
apps1.standardsA: Most non-DeFi apps boil down to three token standards:
apps1.standardsD: (mixed — game items). The "app" is really a smart contract that mints, transfers, or queries these tokens.

cards.title: NFTs & digital ownership
cards.what: A unique on-chain token whose tokenURI points to off-chain metadata (image, JSON). The token proves provenance; the bytes typically live on IPFS or a centralised server.
cards.examples: CryptoPunks (2017) · Bored Ape (2021) · Tickets: Liverpool FC, Coachella · Music: Sound.xyz · Brand IP: Nike RTFKT, Disney pilots.
cards.limit: Most metadata is off-chain — if the URL or IPFS pin disappears, the "NFT" is a broken pointer. The 2021-22 speculation cycle collapsed; survivors pivoted to utility (tickets, rights, identity) rather than JPEG flipping.

cards.title: DAOs & on-chain governance
cards.what: A Decentralized Autonomous Organization is a smart contract that controls a treasury and executes proposals approved by token-holder votes. The contract — not a CEO — moves the funds.
cards.examples: MakerDAO (DAI issuance) · Uniswap DAO (controls $7B+ in UNI) · ENS DAO (.eth registry) · Optimism Citizen House · Gitcoin grants.
cards.limit: Voter turnout often 1–5%. Token-weighted votes mean whales decide. Most "DAOs" delegate decisions to small core teams. Legal status of DAOs is unsettled in most jurisdictions.

apps2.heading: Beyond DeFi — Identity & Gaming
apps2.subtitle: Two more verticals where Ethereum's programmability opens possibilities that centralised systems cannot replicate — yet.

cards.title: Identity & decentralized social
cards.what: Replace email logins with wallet-based identity. ENS gives you a human name (alice.eth) for your address. Sign-In With Ethereum (SIWE) authenticates without passwords. Lens and Farcaster build social graphs on-chain.
cards.examples: ENS: 3M+ names registered · SIWE: 200+ apps integrated · Farcaster: ~300k DAU (2024) · Worldcoin: 7M+ verified humans · Lens: built around composable feed graphs.
cards.limit: Still niche vs Web2 social. Wallet-and-gas onboarding blocks most users. ENS names cost gas to mint. Decentralised social hasn't solved spam or moderation as well as centralised platforms — yet.

cards.title: On-chain gaming & metaverse
cards.sub: Most assets now live on L2s
cards.what: Game assets (skins, characters, items) as tokens players actually own and can trade. Ranges from "off-chain game with NFT cosmetics" to "fully on-chain games" where every game state lives in contracts (Dojo engine).
cards.examples: Immutable (zkEVM for games) · Ronin (Axie Infinity peaked $3B GMV in 2021) · Decentraland · Realms / Loot Survivor on Starknet · DFK Chain (Avalanche L1).
cards.limit: Most "Web3 games" failed (poor gameplay, predatory tokenomics, Ponzi-shaped incentives). Wallet UX still blocks mainstream gaming. Serious studios increasingly land on "game first, blockchain optional".

apps2.uriBody: ERC-721 NFTs typically store only a URL pointing to off-chain metadata. The token on-chain proves you own a pointer; the actual image/asset lives on IPFS, Arweave, or — too often — a single web server. If the host disappears, your NFT renders blank. Always check where the metadata is pinned before buying.

bestFits.heading: Where Ethereum Wins — The Use Cases It Actually Owns
bestFits.subtitleA: Ethereum's design choices (Turing-complete EVM, account model, deep liquidity, ERC standards, the L2 stack) make it the default for anything that needs
bestFits.subtitleStrong: programmable state + composability + an open developer ecosystem

items.name: DeFi & on-chain finance
items.why: Composable money legos — Uniswap, Aave, Compound interoperate by default. ~$50B TVL across L1 + L2.
items.example: Uniswap (DEX), Aave (lending), MakerDAO (DAI), Lido (staking), Curve (stable swaps).

items.name: NFTs & creator economy
items.why: ERC-721 / ERC-1155 are the de-facto standards. Royalty enforcement + provable provenance built in.
items.example: OpenSea, Blur, Sotheby's on-chain auctions · ENS domains · POAPs · proof-of-attendance.

items.name: DAOs & on-chain governance
items.why: Token-weighted voting, programmable treasuries, transparent proposals — battle-tested governance kits.
items.example: Uniswap DAO ($6B treasury), MakerDAO, Arbitrum DAO, Optimism, Gitcoin grants.

items.name: Stablecoins & tokenised RWAs
items.why: Deepest liquidity for USD-backed tokens; RWA issuance via institutional rails (BlackRock BUIDL, Franklin Templeton).
items.example: USDC, USDT, DAI, RLUSD, PYUSD on Ethereum · BlackRock BUIDL ($2B+ AUM) · Ondo Finance.

bestFits.notFitBody: pure store-of-value at Bitcoin-level neutrality (use BTC), high-throughput retail payments without L2 (gas spikes), private enterprise consortia where data must stay off public view (use Fabric), use cases that don't need programmability or composability — extra cost for no benefit.

worstFits.subtitle: Ethereum is the default for anything programmable — but defaults aren't free. There are jobs where its open programmability is the problem, not the solution.

items.name: Pure store of value / digital gold
items.why: ETH has no hard cap, monetary policy changes (Merge, EIP-1559), and a much smaller "sound money" credibility budget vs Bitcoin.
items.alt: Use Bitcoin for neutral, scarce, time-tested SoV.

items.name: Private B2B consortia, regulated data
items.why: Mainnet Ethereum is fully public — bad for KYC data, supplier contracts, healthcare records. Privacy layers (Aztec) help but add complexity and trust assumptions.
items.alt: Use Hyperledger Fabric · Quorum · R3 Corda for permissioned channels.

items.name: Consumer-grade payments on L1
items.why: L1 finalises in ~12 min and fees spike on demand. Even with L2s, withdrawal latency and bridge UX still bite for retail.
items.alt: Use Bitcoin Lightning · Solana · XRPL · Stellar for sub-second payment UX.

items.name: Latency-critical games / real-time apps
items.why: ~12-second blocks on L1, ~1-2 s on most rollups — still too slow for tick-based gaming or live trading. Order-book matching at gas cost is brutal.
items.alt: Use Solana · Sui · Aptos · purpose-built order-book chains (dYdX v4) for latency-bound apps.

worstFits.rightToolBody: Ethereum is unmatched for composable on-chain finance, NFTs, DAOs, identity, and stablecoin issuance. For anything else, ask whether you really need a public global VM — sometimes you don't.

quiz.question: What is the fundamental difference between an Externally Owned Account (EOA) and a Contract Account on Ethereum?
options: EOAs can store ETH; Contract Accounts can only store code and cannot hold a balance.
Contract Accounts can initiate transactions independently; EOAs can only respond when called by a contract.
EOAs are controlled by a private key and can initiate transactions; Contract Accounts are controlled by code and only execute when triggered by an EOA or another contract.
EOAs exist only on Ethereum mainnet; Contract Accounts work across all EVM-compatible chains automatically.
quiz.explanation: This is the foundational distinction of Ethereum's account model. An EOA is what a human controls — it has a private key, can initiate transactions, and pays gas. A Contract Account has no private key — it is pure code deployed at a deterministic address, and it only runs when another account sends it a transaction. This means a Contract Account cannot spontaneously act on a schedule — it needs an EOA (or a keeper bot using an EOA) to trigger it. Every transaction on Ethereum ultimately originates from an EOA.

quiz2.question: An EIP-1559 transaction sets maxFeePerGas = 30 Gwei and maxPriorityFeePerGas = 2 Gwei. The current base fee is 25 Gwei. What does the sender actually pay per gas, and where does each part go?
options: 30 Gwei per gas, all of which is paid to the validator who includes the transaction.
25 Gwei base fee (burned, removed from supply) + 2 Gwei tip (paid to the validator) = 27 Gwei per gas. The remaining 3 Gwei of headroom in maxFeePerGas is refunded to the sender.
25 Gwei base fee paid to the validator + 2 Gwei priority fee burned. Total 27 Gwei per gas.
30 Gwei (max fee) + 2 Gwei (priority fee) = 32 Gwei per gas, paid entirely to the validator.
quiz2.explanation: EIP-1559 split the gas price into two components. The protocol-determined base fee is BURNED (permanently destroyed — this is what makes ETH deflationary when activity is high). The priority fee is the tip the user offers the validator. Effective price per gas is min(maxFeePerGas, baseFee + maxPriorityFeePerGas) — here min(30, 25 + 2) = 27 Gwei. Any unused headroom is refunded. So the sender pays 27 Gwei × gasUsed: 25 burned, 2 to the validator. maxFeePerGas is a cap, not a target.

quiz3.question: Bob deploys a smart contract by sending a transaction with to = null and the compiled bytecode in the data field. How is the new contract's address determined?
options: Bob picks any unused 20-byte address and includes it in the transaction's value field.
The Ethereum protocol generates a random 20-byte address at deployment time, so the address is unpredictable until the transaction is mined.
For a regular CREATE deployment, the address is derived deterministically from keccak256(senderAddress, senderNonce) — Bob can compute the future contract address before sending the transaction.
The address equals keccak256(bytecode), so two deployments of identical contracts always share the same address.
quiz3.explanation: CREATE derives the contract address from rlp(senderAddress, senderNonce), then takes the last 20 bytes of its keccak256 hash. This means anyone can predict the future contract address before deployment — useful for state channels and counterfactual instantiation. CREATE2 (introduced in Constantinople) uses keccak256(0xff, deployer, salt, keccak256(initCode)) so the address depends on a salt and the init code, decoupling it from nonce — enabling address pre-commitment regardless of deployment order. Identical bytecode does NOT share an address with CREATE; CREATE2 distinguishes by salt.

takeaways.title: Section 02 — Key Takeaways
items: Ethereum extends Bitcoin by adding a Turing-complete virtual machine — the EVM
Two account types: Externally Owned Accounts (EOAs) and Contract Accounts
Smart contracts are programs stored on-chain that execute deterministically when triggered
The Merge (2022) replaced Proof of Work with Proof of Stake — cutting energy use by 99.95%
The EVM has become an industry standard — Polygon, Arbitrum, Base, Optimism, and more are EVM-compatible
Bitcoin is digital money; Ethereum is a programmable settlement layer
